data.title: Trump mulls changes to US CHIPS Act terms - report
data.description:
The Trump administration is seeking to renegotiate awards under the U.S. CHIPS and Science Act awards and has indicated delays to some upcoming semiconductor disbursements, Reuters reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

The CHIPS Act brought by the previous Biden administration provides for $39B in grants, billions more in loans and 25% tax credits to boost domestic chip manufacturing and reduce reliance on Asia. Many companies have received awards under the initiative.

The White House is now looking to review the projects awarded. The government plans to renegotiate some of the agreements after evaluating and changing current requirements. However, the extent of the possible changes, and how they could affect agreements already finalized, was not clear. It was also not known if any action has yet been taken, the report added.

“The CHIPS Program Office has told us that certain conditions that do not align with President (Donald) Trump's executive orders and policies are now under review for all CHIPS Direct Funding Agreements,” GlobalWafers's spokesperson Leah Peng told the news agency.

Taiwanese company GlobalWafers' CEO Doris Hsu said that the current status is that the CHIPS Act remains unchanged, and the company has secured contracts worth $406M, according to a separate report by Reuters.

The U.S. Department of Commerce and GlobalWafers did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

"However, at this moment, everything is hypothetical and hasn't happened yet, as we haven't received any notification about required actions. For now, everything is proceeding according to the original plan" Hsu said while speaking to reporters in Taiwan's Hsinchu, the report noted.

Hsu, noted the company has not yet received any funding from the U.S. because it needs to reach certain milestones this year.

"We have a first milestone to achieve, and once we reach it, we will submit the necessary documentation to the relevant authorities for review," Hsu said.

In December 2024, the U.S. Department of Commerce finalized up to $406M in funding for GlobalWafers under the CHIPS Act to ramp up domestic silicon wafer production.

GlobalWafers is not changing its U.S. expansion plan, which is ongoing at three facilities across the country, Hsu noted.

The White House is concerned about several terms underpinning the $39B CHIPS and Science Act industry subsidies, the report noted, citing sources.

These consists of additional clauses, including requirements added into contracts by the Biden administration which say that recipients must use unionized labor to set up factories and help provide affordable childcare for factory workers, the report added.

The White House is also concerned that companies that accepted the subsidies and then announced expansion plans abroad, including in China. The law allowed some investments in China, the report noted.

Intel (INTC), for instance, announced a $300M investment plan in a Chinese assembly and test facility in October 2024, after noting in March that it had won an award under the CHIPS Act.

In November 2025, The U.S. government finalized up to $7.86B in direct funding for Intel (INTC) under the CHIPS Act. Some of the other companies slated to receive funding under the CHIPS Act are Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM), Micron Technology (MU), HP Inc (HPQ), GlobalFoundries (GFS) has South Korean companies Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) and SK hynix.

Many recipients of the CHIPS Act funding have major manufacturing facilities in China, the report added.

Intel disclosed it had received two payments totaling $2.2B in funding under the CHIPS Act, but declined to comment, the report noted.

A spokesperson for TSM said the company received $1.5B monies before the new administration came in as per the milestone terms of its agreement. The spokesperson declined comment on any potential changes to its agreement under the new U.S. government but said the company is continuing to engage with the CHIPS Program Office, the report added.

TSM, Intel, Micron, HP Inc., GlobalFoundries, Samsung and SK hynix did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Magna International Non-GAAP EPS of $1.69, revenue of $10.6B
data.description:
• Magna International press release (NYSE:MGA): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.69.
  • Revenue of $10.6B (+1.4% Y/Y).

  • Outlook Highlights

    • 2025 Sales expected to be between $38.6 billion and $40.2 billion, mainly reflecting negative impacts of foreign currency translation, lower light vehicle production and end of production of Jaguar assembly programs
    • 2025 Adjusted EBIT Margin expected to be between 5.3% and 5.8%
    • Capital spending projected to normalize to historical levels beginning in 2025
    • 2026 Sales expected to grow from 2025 to between $40.5 billion and $42.6 billion
    • 2026 Adjusted EBIT Margin expected to expand to a range of 6.5%-7.2%
    • Expect Free Cash Flow of $1.5 billion or more by 2026

data.title: Starbucks to add 500 new stores in the Middle East in five years - report
data.description:
Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) plans to expand its presence in the Middle East, with CEO Brian Niccol announcing the intention to open about 500 new stores and create 5,000 jobs over the next five years, Bloomberg reported.

The expansion comes as Starbucks seeks to recover from challenges faced in the region, including boycotts from customers over geopolitical tensions. The coffee chain currently has over 1,300 stores in the region.

The CEO initially focused on a plan for the North America business, which brings in about 75% of revenue, and also said that he would reduce management complexity, eliminating layers. These changes could likely result in job cuts, expected by early March.

Also, Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) is eyeing further expansion in China, where Niccol indicated adding a few more thousand stores, according to the report.

data.title: U.S. electricity demand growth through 2027 will be like adding another California
data.description:
How much electricity are we going to need? A lot. Or more precisely, an "unprecedented" 3,500 terawatt hours for the globe over the next three years. That corresponds to adding more than the equivalent of a Japan to the world’s electricity consumption each year, according to the International Energy Agency, which is out with a new report called, "Electricity 2025: Analysis and Forecast to 2027."

Snapshot: "Strong growth in electricity demand is raising the curtain on a new Age of Electricity," per the report. "Electrification of buildings, transportation and industry combined with a growing demand for air conditioners and data centers is ushering a shift toward a global economy with electricity at its foundations." Most of the additional demand for electricity through 2027 will come from emerging and developing economies, led by China, which are expected to make up 85% of the growth.

Perhaps surprisingly, in advanced economies like the U.S., total and per capita demand has stayed relatively flat or even declined since 2009. That's due to increased efficiency across all end-use sectors (like lighting, appliances, and the restructuring/relocation of heavy industries), but things are starting to change amid higher consumption from data centers, EVs, crypto, 5G networks, and heat pumps. In fact, U.S. electricity demand is now estimated to grow at an average annual rate of 2% over the 2025-2027 period, which is equivalent to adding the total electricity demand of California over the next three years.

Power needs: Stocks like Vistra (NYSE:VST), NRG Energy (NYSE:NRG) and Constellation Energy (NASDAQ:CEG) all surged in 2024 as key beneficiaries of electricity demand from the AI revolution. That's set to continue, according to Seeking Alpha Head of Quant Steve Cress, who shares a new list of "Top Stocks For The AI Power Draw." He also points to some quick stats in his latest SA forum, like a "ChatGPT query consumes nearly 10x more electricity than a Google search" and "electricity demands from AI data centers are projected to increase power consumption by 160% over the next decade."

data.title: Euro Area GDP expected to grow 0.1% in Q4
data.description:
The Eurozone economy expanded 0.1% on quarter in the last three months of 2024, better than a flat reading expected, estimates showed. This still remains the weakest economic performance in 2024.

GDP expanded 0.90% in Q4 over the same quarter of the previous year.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: ROKU, ABNB, DKNG, and more
data.description:
Stock futures showed little movement early Friday as markets evaluated President Donald Trump's decision to postpone new reciprocal tariffs and the latest inflation data.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) shares jumped over 14% after posting strong Q4 results, with Gross Booking Value and Nights & Experiences Booked rising 13% and 12%, respectively, both exceeding expectations. Revenue climbed 12%, leading to a profit of $0.73 per share, a sharp rebound from a $0.55 loss a year ago and $0.15 above estimates, while adjusted EBITDA surged 31% to $765M, easily beating the $651.6M consensus. However, the company’s Q1 outlook was muted due to an unfavorable calendar comparison from last year’s Easter timing and Leap Day, which is expected to impact growth by about three percentage points. Airbnb forecasts Q1 revenue to increase 4% to 6% to $2.23B–$2.27B, falling short of the $2.3B consensus, though adjusting for calendar shifts and currency headwinds, revenue is expected to grow 10% to 12%. Looking ahead to 2025, Airbnb plans to invest $200M–$250M in launching and scaling new businesses, with an adjusted EBITDA margin target of at least 34.5%, inclusive of these investments.
  • Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU) shares surged nearly 11% after reporting strong Q4 results and providing a 2025 forecast that met consensus while aiming for profitability in FY2026. The company projects 2025 revenue of $4.61B, slightly above the $4.6B consensus, and adjusted EBITDA of $350M, surpassing the $290.4M estimate. Roku highlighted clear paths to increasing key performance metrics, including streaming hours, platform revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and free cash flow, reaffirming its expectation to achieve full-year operating income profitability in 2026.
  • DraftKings (NASDAQ:DKNG) shares gained 5% after the company reported a 13% revenue increase, fueled by strong customer engagement, expansion into new jurisdictions, a higher structural sportsbook hold percentage, and the acquisition of Jackpocket. Despite customer-friendly NFL season outcomes weighing on results, monthly unique paying customers jumped 36% Y/Y to 4.8M, slightly exceeding the 4.77M consensus. However, average revenue per MUP fell 16% to $97, missing the $197.84 estimate, due to lower ARPMUP for Jackpocket customers and a lower actual sportsbook hold rate. Encouragingly, DraftKings raised its FY2025 revenue guidance midpoint to $6.45B, surpassing the $6.40B consensus, with a new range of $6.3B–$6.6B, further boosting investor confidence.

Biggest stock losers

  • Twilio (NYSE:TWLO) shares dropped 8% after delivering mixed Q4 results and issuing weaker-than-expected Q1 guidance. The company forecasts adjusted EPS of $0.88–$0.93 per share, missing the $0.98 consensus, with revenue expected between $1.13B and $1.14B, the midpoint slightly below the $1.14B estimate. Twilio reaffirmed its long-term financial targets from last month's investor day, projecting 7%–8% organic revenue growth, non-GAAP operating income of $825M–$850M, and free cash flow in the same range.
  • Despite surpassing consensus estimates in FQ1, Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) shares fell 5% as its Q2 revenue outlook missed expectations. The semiconductor equipment maker forecasts an adjusted EPS midpoint of $2.30, slightly above the $2.29 consensus, while projecting total net revenue at a midpoint of $7.1B, falling short of the $7.2B consensus. CFO Brice Hill noted optimism around continued customer investments in advanced technologies but acknowledged headwinds from export control restrictions.

data.title: European markets mostly lower, taking breather from touching record highs
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.30% to 8,740.81

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.24% to 22,558.24, Germany’s wholesale prices rose by 0.9% Y/Y in January.

France (CAC:IND) +0.16% to 8,177.13.

The annual inflation rate in Spain rose to 2.9% in January.

Slovakia’s economy expanded by 1.8% Y/Y in Q4.

Lithuania’s annual inflation rate accelerated to 3.6% in January.

Hungary's construction output declined by 4.2% Y/Y in December.

Switzerland’s producer and import prices fell 0.3% Y/Y in January.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) moved 0.17% lower after gains in the previous session pushed the two benchmarks to record levels. For the week so far, the STOXX 50 soared about 3% and the STOXX 50 added nearly 2%. The euro hovered around $1.04, near two-week highs, as traders weighed President Trump's trade policies and the potential end of the war in Ukraine.

Coming up in the session: Euro Area GDP and employment change expected shortly.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 2 basis points to 4.54%.

Germany's 10-year yield was up 2 basis points to 2.44%.

UK's 10year yield was up 3 basis points to 4.52%.
